stem: You are the paediatric registrar in a regional emergency department reviewing a 5-month-old previously well term girl on day 3 of coryza, cough and fast breathing. She is taking around 60% of her usual breastfeed volumes, has not vomited, and her last wet nappy was 3 hours ago. Initial observations showed mild subcostal recession with SpO2 92% in air; after suction of clear nasal secretions and a 90 mL breastfeed she is alert, smiling, with no recession at rest and SpO2 of 95% in air for 30 minutes. The mother is calm, has a working car, lives 25 minutes from the hospital, has a clear plan to seek review, and there is good GP follow-up the next morning.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, RR 44, SpO2 95% in air (after suction and feed), T 37.6 degrees C, capillary refill under 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Mild wheeze with prolonged expiration, no chest wall recession at rest, good air entry, no focal crackles, mucous membranes moist
data_table.Investigations: Bedside nasopharyngeal aspirate viral PCR pending; no other investigations performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate disposition?

options.text: Admit her for a chest x-ray and intravenous benzylpenicillin until viral nasopharyngeal aspirate PCR results return, given the fever and the possibility of an early bacterial lower respiratory tract co-infection
options.rationale: Routine chest imaging and empirical antibiotics are not indicated in uncomplicated bronchiolitis without focal signs or persistent high fever; both increase harm without altering outcomes in this clinical picture. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge home with a written bronchiolitis action plan, simple analgesia for fever, advice on feed volumes and red-flag symptoms, and review by her general practitioner within 24 hours
options.rationale: She meets discharge criteria: SpO2 at or above 92% in air sustained for at least 30 minutes, mild work of breathing, feeding above 50% of baseline, no apnoeas, reliable carer and access to review; an action plan with red-flag advice and short-interval GP review is standard practice. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit her to the paediatric ward for nasogastric feeding at two-thirds maintenance and continuous pulse oximetry monitoring for at least 24 hours regardless of her current observations and ongoing feeding
options.rationale: Routine nasogastric feeding is reserved for infants taking under 50% of usual oral intake or in respiratory distress that precludes safe oral feeding, neither of which applies here. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge her home only after a 4-hour high-flow nasal cannula trial at 2 L/kg/min in the ED short stay unit to confirm she does not develop any escalating oxygen requirement before going home
options.rationale: High-flow nasal cannula is an escalation therapy for infants with persistent hypoxia or severe work of breathing despite low-flow oxygen, not a pre-discharge screening test for a stable, normoxic infant. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit her for a trial of nebulised hypertonic 3% saline every 6 hours and a single inhaled salbutamol challenge to assess for any bronchodilator-responsive component of her wheeze before discharge
options.rationale: Nebulised hypertonic saline and trial salbutamol are not recommended in routine bronchiolitis management in infants under 12 months and do not change the disposition decision in a stable child meeting discharge criteria. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Disposition in bronchiolitis is driven by sustained SpO2, work of breathing, feeding adequacy, apnoeas and carer factors rather than by interventions of unproven benefit. This infant meets all RCH discharge criteria after simple suction and a feed. Hypertonic saline, bronchodilators, antibiotics and routine imaging are not indicated. Safe discharge requires a clear action plan and short-interval review.
key_points: Discharge thresholds: SpO2 at or above 92% in air sustained 30 minutes, feeds at or above 50% baseline, no apnoea, mild work of breathing
Hypertonic saline and salbutamol are not recommended in routine bronchiolitis under 12 months
Antibiotics and chest x-ray only with focal signs, persistent high fever or severe disease
Carer reliability, distance to care, and follow-up plan are part of the disposition decision
explanation.pearls: If SpO2 sits at 92% in air after suction and feed in an otherwise well infant with reliable carers, discharge with a written action plan and GP review within 24 hours is correct - admitting to escalate to high-flow is over-treatment.
